narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57.4% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings in your school, family income in the lower-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is notably higher at 37.27, while your UGPA actually exceeds their average of 3.24, and full-time enrollment is common at 93.2%. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 1 decile ranking of 1, which stood out as a major factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure since it places you well below the average of 6.04 for those who passed. Next in influence was your UGPA, which worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, reflecting stronger undergraduate performance than typical successful bar takers. Your Year 3 decile of 2 also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less forcefully, as it lags behind the 5.91 average for passers. These three elements—your early academic decile, undergraduate grades, and later decile—dominated the model's thinking, outweighing positives like your solid UGPA and common full-time status. Your lower-middle family income aligns with a smaller but present group of bar passers (about 9.4%), yet it didn't sway the outcome much here. Compared to typical admitted students, who generally show stronger early and mid-undergrad deciles alongside high LSATs, your profile raised more risk flags despite some strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37+ range seen in passers, strengthening your early academic standing through additional coursework or recommendations, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these improvements, and we're here to support your next steps.
